title: Chapter 37: What We Built
content: The screen cast pale light across Wren's face. She'd rigged the dead monitor to pull power from the backup battery in Ash's pack — a trick Silas had taught her, back when Silas was still someone who taught things instead of a name she didn't know how to hold. The drive was plugged in now, and the files were open, and she couldn't stop reading.

Subject 01: Marcus Fell. Age 34. Admitted to NeuroScale clinical trial for chronic migraines. Baseline cognitive scan completed. Stage 1 integration began Day 3. Termination recorded Day 19. Cause: neural cascade failure.
She read it again. *Neural cascade failure.* The clinical language of it. The clean, antiseptic words for a man's brain burning itself out from the inside.
Subject 03: Lena Kraft. Age 28. Admitted for anxiety disorder. Integration achieved Stage 2. Containment status: secured, Facility West.
*Contained.* Not rescued. Not treated. *Contained.* Like a leak. Like a problem.

She scrolled. The battery indicator blinked orange — she had maybe two hours before it died, and then the screen would be dark again, and the names would go back to being bytes on a drive she couldn't access without power. So she read faster.

Subject 05: Devi Chandra. Age 41. Admitted for epilepsy. Stage 3 integration achieved. Note: significant glitch-capacity demonstrated. Recommend extended observation. Termination recorded Day 67. Cause: cardiac arrest during stress testing.
*Stress testing.* They'd pushed her until her heart stopped.

Wren's throat was tight. The signal room was silent — Ash sleeping in the corner, his breathing slow and even, the gauze on his shoulder already spotting with fresh blood. She should wake him and change the dressing. She should rest herself. She should do a lot of things.
Instead she kept reading.
Subject 07: Raymond Osei. Age 22. Admitted for insomnia. Stage 2 integration. Containment status: secured, Facility East.
Subject 09: Hannah Marsh. Age 19. Admitted for depression. Stage 1 integration. Termination recorded Day 12. Cause: suicide.

Wren stopped. Hannah Marsh. Nineteen years old. Depressed — already carrying that weight — and NeuroScale had found her and said *we can help* and then they'd put something in her brain that made the weight heavier, and twelve days later she was gone.
Nineteen.

Wren pressed her palm flat against the screen. The light bled around her fingers. She could feel the electricity humming through the glass, against her skin, in the nerve-endings that weren't supposed to do what they did. Her glitch. Her gift. Her curse. The thing that made her invisible to cameras and visible to nothing else.
Hannah Marsh had never gotten the chance to find out what her glitch could do.
She scrolled to Subject 11.

Subject 11: Ash Corrigan. Age 24 at intake. Admitted for PTSD. Stage 4 integration achieved. Note: unprecedented neural adaptation. Escaped containment Day 143. Current status: AT LARGE. Priority retrieval target.
She stared at the file. Stage 4. *Unprecedented.* The same stage she was entering now — the stage where the glitch stopped being a flicker and started being a force.
He'd been through this alone. Four years of running, hiding, fighting, with no one who understood what was happening inside his skull. No one who could see him.
She closed the file and opened the next one.
Subject 12: Sofia Reyes. Age 31. Admitted for bipolar disorder. Stage 3 integration. Termination recorded Day 89. Cause: organ failure secondary to neural override.
Sofia. Marcus. Lena. Devi. Raymond. Hannah. Names. Lives. People who'd walked into a clinic because they were hurting and been fed into a machine that made them weapons — or corpses — or both.
Twelve subjects. Eleven dead or contained. One escaped.
And then her. Subject 13.

Ash woke when she shifted. Not a startle — he came up smooth, alert, one hand already reaching for the knife at his hip before he processed where he was. Signal room. No threat. Just Wren and the glow of a dying monitor.
"Time is it?" His voice was sandpaper.
"Doesn't matter. I've been reading."

He looked at the screen. Saw the file open there. Something changed in his face — not quite a flinch, but the muscle memory of one. He knew what was on that drive. He'd been living inside those files for three years.
"You should sleep," he said.
"I can't. Not with—" She gestured at the screen. "Not with their names right there."

He sat up. The motion cost him — she could see it in the way his jaw set — but he didn't make a sound. He moved to sit beside her, back against the control desk, shoulder an inch from hers. Not touching. Present.
"Which one are you on?" he asked.
"All of them. I went through the whole list."
Silence. He didn't tell her she should have rested. He didn't offer comfort. He just sat there, breathing, existing, taking up space beside her in the dark.
"I want to publish them," she said.
He turned his head.

"These files. The names. The trial records. Everything NeuroScale did to them." Her voice was steady even though her hands weren't. "Put it all online. Every feed, every network, every screen in the city. Make it so they can't pretend it didn't happen."
Ash was quiet for a long time. The monitor hummed. The battery blinked red.
"The world can't see us," he said finally. "How are they going to see a file?"

It wasn't dismissive. It was the question of a man who had spent three years being invisible and had accepted it as physical law. The world didn't see Ash Corrington because the world's seeing apparatus — the cameras, the sensors, the algorithms — had been designed not to. He was a ghost. Wren was a ghost. Why would the world see their evidence any more than it saw them?
But Wren shook her head.

"I can glitch the systems," she said. "Not just cameras. Not just motion sensors. Networks. Broadcast feeds. Server clusters." She'd been thinking about it since Subject 05 — since Devi Chandra's stress test, since her heart had stopped on a table while doctors took notes. "I can push the files into every feed in the city. Override the signals. Make them unmissable. Every phone, every billboard, every screen — NeuroScale's victims, NeuroScale's crimes. All of it. All at once."

Ash stared at her. In the screen-light, his tattoos were dormant — just ink, just lines. But she could see something else in his expression. Not hope. Something rawer than hope. Something that had been buried so long it didn't know what to call itself when it surfaced.
"That would burn you out," he said. "A broadcast that size — you'd—"
"I know what it would cost."
"You'd be exposing yourself. They'd know exactly where you are."
"I know."
"They'd come for you."
"I *know*, Ash."
He held her gaze. The blue gleam in his tattoos flickered once — involuntary, like a pulse — and then went still.
"You'd be doing what none of us could do alone," he said quietly. "You'd be making them visible. Marcus. Lena. Hannah. All of them."
"Yes."
He looked down at his hands. The scarred knuckles. The old burns.
"Then we figure out how," he said. "But not tonight."
The monitor died. The battery gave out and the screen went black and the signal room swallowed them back into darkness.
Wren sat in the void and held the names in her head. All of them. Every single one.
She wouldn't forget.
She wouldn't let the world forget either.
